Synthetic Canadians: Comparing Synthetic Survey Data Generators

2019 Canadian Election Study — Political Attitudes Validation Study

Author

Synthetic Opinion Lab

Published

March 15, 2026

Overview

This report validates three synthetic data generation approaches against the 2019 Canadian Election Study (CES19), conducted during the federal election (n = 302).

The 20 variables span six substantive domains: democratic satisfaction, political efficacy, populism, institutional confidence (4 institutions), immigration attitudes, social conservatism, and economic inequality, plus party identification and vote choice.

Three generators are compared:

Generator N synthetic Overall quality score
LLM (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) 100 0.586
IRT (Graded Response Model) 1,000 0.486
Agent (Opinion dynamics) 500 0.485

Correlation Structure

Frobenius distance from real correlation matrix:
  IRT                 : Frobenius = 5.04, r(lower tri) = 0.096
  LLM                 : Frobenius = 4.89, r(lower tri) = 0.309
  Agent               : Frobenius = 11.46, r(lower tri) = 0.023

Regression Replication: Vote Choice Predictors

Summary

Dimension Real IRT LLM Agent
N respondents 302 1,000 100 500
Overall quality score — 0.486 0.586 0.485
Valid response range ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅
Mean-level accuracy — Partial Good Partial
Variance accuracy — Good Moderate Moderate
Correlation structure — Moderate Best Weak

LLM achieves the highest fidelity (0.586). IRT (0.486) produces good variance. Agent (0.485) converges to moderate uniform intensity. Note: CES19 uses the hybrid CPS (campaign) + PES (post-election) design with mixed 4/5/8-point scales.
